title: Blood on Wool
stainChemistry: Classification: Protein. Blood contains hemoglobin—a quaternary protein with four globin chains surrounding an iron-containing heme group (iron+porphyrin ring chromophore). When blood contacts wool at room temperature, hemoglobin bonds to wool keratin (the wool fiber protein itself) through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic effects. The critical difference from cotton: wool IS protein. Protein stains bond to fibers via peptide-to-fiber hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. Heat denatures the protein, cross-linking it to the substrate and making removal extremely difficult. Treatment: cold water flush to prevent heat-setting, followed by neutral detergent and reducing agents safe for protein fibers.
whyThisWorks: Blood stains bond directly to wool keratin (both are protein structures — it's like-on-like). Cold water prevents the protein from heat-setting permanently. NSD is safe for protein fibers because it won't attack the fiber itself. Enzyme-based digestants are prohibited on wool because they digest the wool fiber along with the stain. Sodium hydrosulfite provides safe reducing action to break down residual discoloration without the oxidative risk that peroxide poses to protein fibers.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) at neutral setting and gently work into the blood stain. Dwell 5–10 minutes. NSD is safe for wool and silk because it won't attack the fiber. Never use enzyme-based digestants on protein fibers.

2. Apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) to break down any remaining blood discoloration. Dwell 5–10 minutes. This reducing agent is the only safe bleaching option for wool and silk — never use hydrogen peroxide or oxidizing bleach on protein fibers.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all chemical residue. Dwell 3–5 minutes. Rinse with cool water — never use warm or hot water on blood stains or on wool.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue and restore the fiber's natural state. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry gently — never wring wool.
products: Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for protein fibers)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat denatures hemoglobin and cross-links it to the wool keratin fiber, making removal impossible. Never use warm or hot water at any stage.
warnings: Using HOT water or enzyme detergent — both destroy the wool fiber.
Machine washing before spotting — combines heat, agitation, and enzymes and will permanently felt the garment.
Rubbing or aggressive scrubbing — causes felting and damages the fiber surface sheen.
Leaving acidic or bleaching solutions on the wool too long — slow hydrolysis of keratin continues over hours.
Using high concentrations of peroxide or vinegar — exceeds wool's pH tolerance.
NEVER use bleach, chlorine, protease, or enzyme-based spotters on wool.

steps: 1. Rinse with cold water right away. Blot gently with a clean white cloth — don't rub. Cold water keeps the stain from setting; rubbing pushes it deeper into the fabric.
2. Mix a small squirt of dish soap (Dawn or similar) into cold water. Blot the solution gently onto the stain. Soap breaks down the stain so it lifts off the fabric.
3. Let it sit for 5–10 minutes. Don't use any heat. Time lets the cleaner work; heat locks protein stains in permanently.
4. Blot gently with a clean white cloth until the stain fades. Each pass pulls more of the stain out of the fabric.
5. Rinse gently with cold water and lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing. Heat and agitation damage delicate fibers.
products: Dawn dish soap (or similar dish soap)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two treatment attempts, take it to a professional. Wool is too delicate to risk aggressive home treatments. A professional cleaner has specialized agents that are safe for this material and more effective than household products.
warnings: Test on an inside seam or hidden area first — delicate fabrics can react unpredictably.
Never use bleach, enzyme cleaners, or hot water on wool.
Never wring or twist wool — gently press out moisture between towels.
Lay flat to dry to keep the garment's shape.

neverDo: Never use hot water — permanently sets blood protein into wool keratin.
Never use enzyme-based cleaners or digestants — enzymes dissolve wool fiber along with the stain.
Never use chlorine bleach or oxidizing bleach — destroys wool fiber structure.
Never machine wash before spotting — heat, agitation, and detergent enzymes will permanently felt the garment.
Never use alcohol or alcohol-based products — permanently sets protein stains.
fiberSensitivities: Wool is a protein fiber — enzyme cleaners attack it the same way they attack the stain.
Wool felts with heat and agitation — always handle gently, cold, and lay flat to dry.
Wool has a limited pH tolerance — avoid high concentrations of acids or alkalis.
Acetic acid is safe at dilute concentrations (1:10) but prolonged exposure hydrolyzes keratin.

escalation.when: For old, dried blood stains on wool, do not use enzyme detergents. Pre-soak in cool water for 30 minutes to soften the stain, then proceed with neutral detergent and reducing agent steps. For very stubborn stains, professional dry cleaning is safer than repeated home treatment, which compounds damage risk.
escalation.whatToTell: This one took some work — the blood stain on your garment needed a multi-step treatment to break it down. Blood stains set deeper with heat and time — the earlier it's treated professionally, the better the result. The treated area may continue to improve over the next day or two as it fully dries.
escalation.specialistType: wet cleaner specializing in delicate wool garments

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Was the garment washed or put in the dryer before this treatment attempt?
Is the wool a fine gauge (sweater, knitwear) or a woven structure (blazer, trousers)?
Has any product already been applied to the stain — and if so, what?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: A fresh stain on undisturbed wool responds well to NSD alone. A heat-set or machine-washed stain may require sodium hydrosulfite to break down cross-linked protein. Fine-gauge knitwear requires even shorter dwell times and lighter touch to prevent felting. Prior enzyme treatment may have caused fiber damage that limits recovery.

timeEstimate: 10–20 min (fresh); 20–40 min for dried stains requiring multiple passes
